---
title: "Fraud Analysis Dashboard"
subtitle: "Quarto starter with sample visuals and an embedded AI assistant"
format: dashboard
resources:
  - scripts/chat.js
  - styles/dashboard.css
  - data/*.csv
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

# Import pandas for data loading and shaping
import pandas as pd
# Import plotly express for interactive charts
import plotly.express as px

# Read the sample datasets used by each dashboard page
open_payments = pd.read_csv("data/open_payments.csv")
# Read the supplier dataset for spend and risk visuals
suppliers = pd.read_csv("data/suppliers.csv")
# Read the referring provider dataset for referral visuals
referring_providers = pd.read_csv("data/referring_providers.csv")
# Read the risk signal dataset for case and exposure visuals
risk_signals = pd.read_csv("data/risk_signals.csv")

# Define a shared chart style helper so visuals stay consistent

def style_plot(fig):
    # Update the plot to match the dark dashboard theme
    fig.update_layout(
        paper_bgcolor="#1e293b",
        plot_bgcolor="#1e293b",
        font=dict(color="#e2e8f0"),
        margin=dict(l=30, r=20, t=60, b=30),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, xanchor="right", x=1),
    )
    # Refine axis styling for readability against the dark background
    fig.update_xaxes(showgrid=False, zeroline=False)
    # Refine axis styling for readability against the dark background
    fig.update_yaxes(gridcolor="rgba(148, 163, 184, 0.18)", zeroline=False)
    # Return the styled chart object
    return fig
```

# Open Payments

## Row {height="34%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Open Payments Snapshot"
#| output: asis

# Calculate the total open payments amount across all categories and regions
open_payment_total = open_payments["amount_m"].sum()
# Calculate the total number of records used in the sample view
open_payment_records = open_payments["payment_count"].sum()
# Calculate the average risk index for the page summary cards
open_payment_risk = open_payments["risk_index"].mean()

# Print a compact metric layout that echoes the original card-based design
print(f"""
<div class='metric-grid'>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Total Payments</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>${open_payment_total:,.1f}M</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Sample modeled amount</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Payment Records</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{open_payment_records:,.0f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Across four regions</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Avg. Risk Index</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{open_payment_risk:.1f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Scaled 0 to 100</div>
  </div>
</div>
""")
```

## Row {height="66%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Payments by Category"

# Aggregate payment totals by payment category for the bar chart
payments_by_category = (
    open_payments
    .groupby("payment_category", as_index=False)["amount_m"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("amount_m", ascending=False)
)

# Build the category comparison bar chart
fig = px.bar(
    payments_by_category,
    x="payment_category",
    y="amount_m",
    text_auto=".1f",
    labels={"payment_category": "Category", "amount_m": "Amount (Millions)"},
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| title: "Regional Share of Open Payments"

# Aggregate payment totals by region for the pie chart
payments_by_region = (
    open_payments
    .groupby("region", as_index=False)["amount_m"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("amount_m", ascending=False)
)

# Build the regional distribution pie chart
fig = px.pie(
    payments_by_region,
    names="region",
    values="amount_m",
    hole=0.45,
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

# Suppliers

## Row {height="34%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Supplier Snapshot"
#| output: asis

# Calculate total supplier spend from the sample data
supplier_spend_total = suppliers["total_spend_m"].sum()
# Calculate total high-risk orders from the sample data
supplier_risk_orders = suppliers["high_risk_orders"].sum()
# Calculate the weighted average supplier risk score
supplier_avg_risk = suppliers["supplier_risk_score"].mean()

# Print metric cards for the supplier page
print(f"""
<div class='metric-grid'>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Supplier Spend</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>${supplier_spend_total:,.1f}M</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Across strategic vendors</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>High-Risk Orders</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{supplier_risk_orders:,.0f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Flagged in sample history</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Avg. Supplier Risk</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{supplier_avg_risk:.1f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Scaled 0 to 100</div>
  </div>
</div>
""")
```

## Row {height="66%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Top Supplier Spend"

# Select the highest-spend suppliers for the bar chart
supplier_spend = suppliers.sort_values("total_spend_m", ascending=False)

# Build the supplier spend comparison bar chart
fig = px.bar(
    supplier_spend,
    x="supplier_name",
    y="total_spend_m",
    color="supplier_tier",
    text_auto=".1f",
    labels={"supplier_name": "Supplier", "total_spend_m": "Spend (Millions)"},
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| title: "Spend Mix by Product Family"

# Aggregate supplier spend by product family for the pie chart
spend_by_family = (
    suppliers
    .groupby("product_family", as_index=False)["total_spend_m"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("total_spend_m", ascending=False)
)

# Build the product-family distribution pie chart
fig = px.pie(
    spend_by_family,
    names="product_family",
    values="total_spend_m",
    hole=0.45,
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

# Referring Providers

## Row {height="34%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Provider Snapshot"
#| output: asis

# Calculate total referred claims represented in the page
provider_claims_total = referring_providers["referred_claims"].sum()
# Calculate total flagged referrals represented in the page
provider_flagged_total = referring_providers["flagged_referrals"].sum()
# Calculate the average referral risk score for page context
provider_avg_risk = referring_providers["provider_risk_score"].mean()

# Print metric cards for the provider page
print(f"""
<div class='metric-grid'>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Referred Claims</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{provider_claims_total:,.0f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Across high-volume referrers</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Flagged Referrals</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{provider_flagged_total:,.0f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Triggered review rules</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Avg. Provider Risk</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{provider_avg_risk:.1f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Scaled 0 to 100</div>
  </div>
</div>
""")
```

## Row {height="66%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Referrals by Specialty"

# Aggregate claim volume by referring specialty for the bar chart
claims_by_specialty = (
    referring_providers
    .groupby("specialty", as_index=False)["referred_claims"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("referred_claims", ascending=False)
)

# Build the specialty comparison bar chart
fig = px.bar(
    claims_by_specialty,
    x="specialty",
    y="referred_claims",
    text_auto=True,
    labels={"specialty": "Specialty", "referred_claims": "Referred Claims"},
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| title: "Flagged Referrals by State"

# Aggregate flagged referrals by state for the pie chart
flagged_by_state = (
    referring_providers
    .groupby("state", as_index=False)["flagged_referrals"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("flagged_referrals", ascending=False)
)

# Build the state distribution pie chart
fig = px.pie(
    flagged_by_state,
    names="state",
    values="flagged_referrals",
    hole=0.45,
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

# Risk Signals

## Row {height="34%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Risk Signal Snapshot"
#| output: asis

# Calculate total open cases across all signal types
risk_cases_total = risk_signals["open_cases"].sum()
# Calculate total financial exposure represented in the sample data
risk_exposure_total = risk_signals["exposure_m"].sum()
# Calculate the average rule confidence score for the page summary
risk_confidence_avg = risk_signals["rule_confidence"].mean()

# Print metric cards for the risk signal page
print(f"""
<div class='metric-grid'>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Open Cases</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{risk_cases_total:,.0f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Across active signals</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Exposure</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>${risk_exposure_total:,.1f}M</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Estimated recoverable amount</div>
  </div>
  <div class='metric-tile'>
    <div class='metric-label'>Avg. Rule Confidence</div>
    <div class='metric-value'>{risk_confidence_avg:.1f}</div>
    <div class='metric-sub'>Scaled 0 to 100</div>
  </div>
</div>
""")
```

## Row {height="66%"}

```{python}
#| title: "Open Cases by Signal Type"

# Sort signal categories by open case volume for the bar chart
signal_cases = risk_signals.sort_values("open_cases", ascending=False)

# Build the signal comparison bar chart
fig = px.bar(
    signal_cases,
    x="signal_type",
    y="open_cases",
    color="severity_band",
    text_auto=True,
    labels={"signal_type": "Signal Type", "open_cases": "Open Cases"},
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| title: "Exposure by Severity Band"

# Aggregate exposure by severity band for the pie chart
exposure_by_severity = (
    risk_signals
    .groupby("severity_band", as_index=False)["exposure_m"]
    .sum()
    .sort_values("exposure_m", ascending=False)
)

# Build the severity mix pie chart
fig = px.pie(
    exposure_by_severity,
    names="severity_band",
    values="exposure_m",
    hole=0.45,
)
# Apply shared dashboard styling to the chart
fig = style_plot(fig)
# Render the chart to the dashboard card
fig.show()
```

# AI Assistant

## Row {height="100%"}

::: {.card title="AI Fraud Assistant"}
<div class="assistant-shell">
  <div class="assistant-intro">
    <div class="assistant-title">Ask about payment trends, supplier anomalies, provider patterns, or risk signals</div>
    <div class="assistant-subtitle">This page reuses the chat experience from the original static HTML and can point to the existing backend endpoint.</div>
  </div>
  <div class="assistant-suggestions">
    <div class="suggestion-chip">Summarize the highest-risk supplier patterns</div>
    <div class="suggestion-chip">Compare referral spikes by state</div>
    <div class="suggestion-chip">Explain the main open payment outliers</div>
  </div>
  <div class="chat-window" id="chat-window">
    <div class="message assistant-message">
      <strong>Assistant:</strong> Hello; ask about the sample dashboard trends or wire this pane to the existing Gemini backend.
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="chat-input-row">
    <input id="chat-input" type="text" placeholder="Ask about fraud risk patterns..." />
    <button id="chat-send" type="button">Send</button>
  </div>
  <div class="assistant-note">CUSTOMIZE: update the chat endpoint in <code>scripts/chat.js</code> if the backend URL changes.</div>
</div>
<script src="scripts/chat.js"></script>
:::
